ci-terminal-map-base.title: Create the terminal map folders
ci-terminal-map-base.prompt:
Create `terminal-map/labs`, `terminal-map/notes`, and `terminal-map/archive`.

Use the terminal. The editor is hidden on purpose for this task.
ci-terminal-map-base.hint: Use terminal commands such as `pwd`, `ls`, `mkdir -p`, `touch`, `cp`, `mv`, or `rm` depending on the task.

help.concept: This is a Linux terminal workspace task. Complete it by changing the file tree with terminal commands.
help.hint_1: Use `pwd` and `ls` to inspect where you are.
help.hint_2: The checker looks for the required folders/files and checks that forbidden files are gone.

ci-terminal-map-base.placeholder: Use the terminal to complete this task.

project-goal.title: Your goal is a clear terminal map
project-goal.bodyMarkdown:
In this project, you will build a small folder map. The goal is not to write a script. The goal is to use terminal commands confidently.

You will create folders for labs, notes, and archive files, then place marker files in the right locations.

project-workflow.title: Work in small command steps
project-workflow.bodyMarkdown:
A good project workflow is:

```bash
pwd
mkdir -p terminal-map/labs terminal-map/notes terminal-map/archive
touch terminal-map/notes/readme.txt
ls terminal-map
```

Run one or two commands, inspect the result, then continue.

project-checking.title: The checker looks at your workspace
project-checking.bodyMarkdown:
When you click Check Answer, Zoeskoul checks the file tree created by your terminal commands.

For terminal projects, the important question is: did the right folders and files exist in the workspace?
